# Chapter 17

[c4m9t2h7] The truck smelled like blood and coolant and that sweet plastic stink the airbags left behind, a scent like melted toys. Wrench lay half on his side in the back seat, wedged into blankets Diesel had packed tight, his leg wrong in a way Flynn couldn’t look at for long. The implant at the base of his skull kept chirping soft offers—pain smoothing, mood correction, streaming prayers—until Diesel slapped the interface dark again. Flynn held Wrench’s hand and felt the tremor under skin, the body trying to believe in its own continuity.

[0j7n3q8v] Cage drove ahead in the Charger like nothing had happened, taillights taped to slits, the car’s silhouette clean against the first gray of morning. Every so often he blipped the throttle and the sound rolled back over the empty road like laughter. Birdie’s head leaned out the window, hair in the wind, scanning for watchers that weren’t there, and when he saw the truck he lifted a fist like a victory sign. Flynn watched that gesture and thought about how easy it was to call survival a win when you weren’t the one bleeding.

[6s1p4x0k] Diesel took them off the main highway as soon as the sun broke, down a frontage road that ran along the solar fields. The panels stood in rows like dark graves, each one drinking light. Maintenance drones moved between them with little blinking eyes, indifferent as insects. Flynn imagined the drones logging plates, heat signatures, faces, saving them in some corporate memory for later sale. Cage didn’t seem to care. He wanted an audience, even if the audience was just a machine.

[w8b2f6d1] They slid into the abandoned subdivision by midmorning, streets half-buried in sand, streetlights bent like old men, stucco shells with windows punched out by wind and time. The place had been planned for families who never came. The cul-de-sacs were bowls full of tumbleweeds. Diesel parked the truck inside a garage with no door and killed the engine. The sudden quiet felt obscene. Somewhere out in the open a crow laughed.

[2r5z9m1q] Wrench started making noise once the engine stopped, low and animal, the pain finding room now that the road was still. Diesel and Flynn hauled him out on a tarp and set him on a carpet of dust where a living room used to be. The house had no roof. Sun poured in like judgment. Diesel checked his neck port and the implant lights. Green, then amber, then green again, like a liar changing stories. Wrench’s eyes rolled and found Flynn for a second. There was no gratitude in them, only fear.

[n3v0k7s6] Cage wandered in after them as if arriving to a party he’d planned. He had a bottle of water in one hand and a bag of jerky in the other. He looked clean, like violence hadn’t touched him. He crouched near Wrench and studied the bent knee with interest, the way a mechanic looks at a cracked bracket. “That’s gonna take,” he said. “But he’ll walk.” Diesel stared at him. Cage glanced up, amused. “What,” he said. “You want me to cry.”

[9l2a5y8c] Flynn heard his own voice before he decided to use it. “You hit him,” he said. The words sounded small in the broken house. Cage’s face didn’t change. Birdie appeared in the doorway behind him, chewing, eyes bright. Diesel didn’t move. Cage said, “I nudged him.” Flynn took a step closer. He could smell the road on Cage, hot rubber and sun. “You tried to kill him.” Cage shrugged. “He’s alive.”

[h7q1u4e9] Flynn’s hands were shaking and he hated that Cage could see it. “He’s not alive like he was,” Flynn said. He gestured toward Wrench, toward the ruined angle of bone, the slack mouth. “He’s never going to be the same.” Cage looked at Wrench again, then back at Flynn, patient as a teacher with a dumb student. “Neither will we,” Cage said. “That’s the point. You think you stay clean? You think you do a thing and it doesn’t change you? We’re becoming something else.”

[f2d8k0p3] Diesel made a sound like she was about to speak and swallowed it. Birdie stepped forward, shoulders tight. “Wrench was going to bounce,” Birdie said, defensive before anyone accused him. “He was gonna take his cut and talk. You heard him. He was scared.” Flynn looked at Birdie and saw the kid’s eyes searching for permission, for an adult to tell him the world still made sense. Cage didn’t even look at Birdie. Cage didn’t need help. The kid offered loyalty the way a dog offers its throat.

[m6x4c9b2] Flynn stared at Cage and felt the ground shift under him, the old story collapsing. He’d thought Cage was reckless, a thrill addict, a man shaped wrong but still human. Now he saw the calm behind it. The choice. Cage had decided Wrench was a threat and then he’d removed him like a part that didn’t fit. He hadn’t done it in anger. He’d done it with the same steady hands he used on a carburetor. Flynn realized Cage was insane in a way the world rewarded.

[1a9n7t5r] Cage stood and stretched, joints popping, and it sounded like applause in the empty room. “We’re burning daylight,” he said. “We rest, we roll tonight, we’re over the line by dawn.” Diesel’s eyes stayed on him. “Mexico,” she said, and it came out as an accusation. Cage smiled. “La Carrera,” he said, as if naming a god. He looked at Flynn like he expected worship too. “You want to be remembered,” he said. “This is where you do it.”

[v9e3s2j8] Diesel turned away first. She walked out through the missing wall into the yard that used to have grass and now had sand and beer cans and a dead swing set. Flynn followed her because he didn’t know what else to do. In the shade of a mesquite she knelt by a toolbox she’d stashed under a tarp. She pulled it free and opened it. Inside were map printouts, fuel cards, a burner phone, and a wad of cash wrapped in tape. She didn’t look up when she said, “We can leave.”

[5g0w6n4y] Flynn blinked. “Leave,” he repeated, like it was a foreign word. Diesel finally met his eyes. In that look was exhaustion and resolve and something like fear. “I’ve been building something,” she said. “Not for him. For me.” She led Flynn to the next lot over where a garage door hung crooked and half buried. Behind it, under a canvas sheet, a car shape waited. Diesel tugged the sheet back and sunlight hit primer-gray metal. A ’68 Mustang fastback, stripped and rebuilt, quiet and predatory.

[t8h5q1z0] “She runs?” Flynn asked. His voice had a hope in it he didn’t trust. Diesel nodded. “She runs clean,” she said. “No tags, no trackers. I kept the electronics dumb. Analog where it counts.” She popped the hood and showed him the old V8 sitting there like a heart. “Enough fuel to make it to Flagstaff, trade out, keep going,” she said. “We disappear into the boring parts of the world. We get jobs. We sleep.” She said sleep like it was a miracle.

[p1v3d7s8] Flynn’s mind filled with the image of a room with a door that locked, a bed with sheets, a morning where no one screamed. He wanted it so badly he felt sick. Then Wrench moaned inside the ruined house, and Cage’s laughter drifted over the street, and Flynn’s chest tightened. “What about Birdie,” Flynn said. He hated himself for making it a question. Diesel’s jaw clenched. “Bring him,” she said. “If he’ll come.”

[q6k2m8f9] They found Birdie sitting on a curb in front of a house with no roof, the Charger parked behind him like a black animal at rest. He was cleaning a pistol with careful hands, copying movements he’d seen in videos. Cage sat in the driver’s seat with the door open, boots on the sand, studying a paper map like it was scripture. When Flynn approached, Cage looked up and smiled, welcoming. Birdie’s eyes flicked between them, wary.

[z0r7n5l4] “Birdie,” Flynn said, keeping his voice low. “Need to talk. Just us.” Birdie didn’t stand. “Why,” he said. Cage’s smile widened. Diesel stayed back, arms crossed, a wall. Flynn tried again. “It’s about Wrench.” At the name, Birdie’s face hardened like a mask snapping into place. “Wrench was gonna sell us,” Birdie said. “He was weak.” He said weak the way Cage said it, like it was a diagnosis.

[s3d9a1x6] Flynn crouched so he was level with the kid. Up close Birdie looked younger, freckles under dust, a bruise on his knuckle from working too hard on something that resisted. “You saw him in there,” Flynn said. “You saw what happened.” Birdie’s eyes darted toward the roofless house where Wrench lay, then back. “He’ll heal,” Birdie said quickly. “Revival does it. That’s what it’s for.” Flynn shook his head. “It doesn’t put you back,” he said. “It just keeps you here.”

[b8k4v0q1] Cage spoke without raising his voice. “Don’t poison him,” he said. Flynn looked at him. Cage’s gaze was calm, almost kind. “He’s figuring it out,” Cage said. “Let him.” Birdie straightened, angry now, confused. “What are you doing,” he snapped at Flynn, and the betrayal in his tone cut Flynn more than a threat would have. “You trying to make me scared.” Flynn opened his mouth and nothing came out that didn’t sound like pleading.

[4n2c7m5p] Diesel stepped in. “We’re leaving tonight,” she said, blunt as a hammer. Birdie’s head turned toward her. “Who is we.” Diesel didn’t flinch. “Me and Flynn,” she said. Cage laughed softly. “No you’re not,” he said. Diesel stared at him like she was measuring distances. Cage leaned back in the seat and rested a hand on the steering wheel. “You can go,” he said. “But you won’t. You love this too much. And you love him.” He nodded at Flynn, as if naming property.

[j5s0u8t2] Flynn felt the street tilt. His mouth went dry. Diesel’s eyes flicked to him—warning, invitation, last chance. He saw the Mustang in his head like a door opening. He saw Birdie’s thin shoulders, his careful hands on a gun, his hunger for a father. He saw Cage’s grin, the inevitability of it. If Flynn left, Birdie would stay. If Flynn stayed, maybe he could keep the kid alive a little longer, keep him from taking the worst lessons all at once. Maybe was the word that killed him.

[x7f3l9n0] “I can’t,” Flynn said. It came out hoarse. Diesel’s face didn’t change, but something in her eyes dimmed. “You can,” she said. “You’re choosing not to.” Flynn shook his head. “I can’t leave him,” he said, and he didn’t know if he meant Birdie or the version of himself that still wanted to believe he could fix things. Cage watched, pleased, like he’d predicted a coin toss and won. Birdie looked between them, breathing fast, like a kid watching adults decide his fate.

[e6p1r4w7] Diesel let out a slow breath. “Then you’re choosing to burn with Cage,” she said. She said it gently, which was worse. Flynn’s throat tightened. “Maybe I can still—” he began. Diesel cut him off with a small shake of her head. “You can’t save him,” she said. “You can’t change Cage. You can only survive.” She looked past Flynn at the Charger, at the man inside it, at the road south. Then she picked up her toolbox and walked back toward the ruined house anyway, because she couldn’t leave the kid alone with the monster, and because leaving was never as simple as driving away.

[9w6m2t8k] By afternoon the subdivision baked. Heat shimmered off the cracked asphalt and made the air look like water. They moved Wrench to the shade and fed him sips through a straw. He drifted in and out, sometimes crying without tears, sometimes staring at the sky like he was watching something fall. Cage laid out the Mexico route on the hood of the Charger and talked about border gaps and bribe prices and the race like it was a pilgrimage. Birdie listened with his whole body. Flynn listened too, because he’d stayed, and because staying meant learning the liturgy.

[g9t1c3r6] Diesel worked Wrench’s leg with the same hands she used on engines, firm and exact, as if pain was just a stubborn bolt. She set the knee the best she could and wrapped it tight with strips torn from a curtain. Wrench screamed once and then went silent, eyes wide, breath coming in thin whistles. Flynn held his shoulders down and felt the man’s bones shake under him. Diesel didn’t apologize. “If it swells wrong you lose it,” she said. Her voice was flat, but Flynn saw the tremble at the corner of her mouth.

[u0b7m2s4] After, Flynn walked the empty street alone. The houses were sun-bleached boxes, each one with the same floorplan like a repeated mistake. In a driveway a child’s bike lay half-buried, wheels still, handlebars twisted. Flynn stopped by the lot where Diesel’s Mustang hid under canvas and he didn’t touch it, just stood there and listened to his own breathing. He tried to imagine leaving in it with Diesel—north, then west, then anywhere that didn’t know Cage’s name. The imagination felt like theft.

[l8q3h6v1] Cage called him back with a whistle, friendly as a dog owner. Flynn found Cage and Birdie unloading gear from the Charger—fuel cans, a crate of parts, packets of electrolyte gel with corporate logos still bright. Cage tossed one to Flynn. “Eat,” he said. “You’ll need it.” Flynn caught it and held the slick packet like it was a verdict. Cage leaned close enough that Flynn could smell mint gum over exhaust. “You think you’re the first kid to look at me like that,” Cage said softly. “You’re not special. But you can learn.”

[r1n9d0x5] Flynn went back to Wrench at dusk. The man was half-asleep, mouth moving around words that wouldn’t form. When his eyes opened they were wet, lucid for a heartbeat. “Don’t,” Wrench rasped, and Flynn didn’t know if it meant don’t go, don’t stay, don’t believe. Flynn squeezed his hand and felt the grip weak, like a signal fading. Outside, engines ticked as they cooled. The desert waited. Flynn sat there until the light left, as if time in the room could be hoarded and spent later.

[k2y7p0d5] When the sun went down, they built a small fire in a broken bathtub and the flames threw orange onto the raw studs and exposed rebar, turning the subdivision into a skeleton church. Cage sat closest to the heat, face lit, eyes bright, telling Birdie stories about racers who’d died smiling, about engines that seized and kept going on momentum alone, about men who crossed lines they couldn’t uncross and called it freedom. Diesel sat across the fire, quiet, hands busy with Wrench’s splint, listening like someone forced to hear her own sentence read aloud. Flynn watched the flames and thought of the Mustang hidden under canvas and of the road that might have been. The kid leaned in and asked Cage questions the way Flynn once had, and Cage answered with warmth that felt like love until you saw the teeth in it.
